claim: SARBayes MapScore framework evaluates probability maps of lost person location against historical searches; the ISRID Distance Ring model scored 0.78 (95%CI: 0.74-0.82, n=376 cases), while a Combined Distance+Watershed model scored 0.81 (95%CI: 0.77-0.84).
stance_target: Bayesian probability maps can be validated empirically against historical SAR data
scope_conditions: Wilderness SAR (WiSAR), human lost persons, ISRID database
quoted_support: On a metric where random maps score 0 and perfect maps score 1, the ISRID Distance Ring model scored 0.78 (95%CI: 0.74-0.82, on 376 cases). The Combined model was slightly better at .81 (95%CI: 0.77-0.84).

claim: Lost person spatial distribution in WiSAR follows a lognormal distance model fit to ISRID Euclidean distance data by subject category, enabling a prior probability distribution for location.
stance_target: Formal prior distributions over lost subject location can be derived from historical incident databases
scope_conditions: Wilderness SAR, human lost persons, ISRID subject categories
quoted_support: Examining the Euclidean Distance data for different categories, it was found that a lognormal curve roughly captured the shape of the data. The Log-Normal (LN) is a two parameter distribution which assumes that the logarithm of your data follows a normal distribution.

claim: Sava, Twardy, Koester, and Sonwalkar (2016) published 'Evaluating Lost Person Behavior Models' in Transactions in GIS, providing a peer-reviewed methodology for validating behavioral prediction models against ISRID data.
stance_target: Formal published framework exists for evaluating lost person behavioral models using historical SAR data
quoted_support: Sava, E., Twardy, C., Koester, R., & Sonwalkar, M. (2016). Evaluating Lost Person Behavior Models. Transactions in GIS, 20(1), 38–53. http://doi.org/10.1111/tgis.12143

claim: SAR incident time follows a von Mises distribution (circular distribution) centered near 5:30pm, indicating time-of-day patterns in lost person behavior that can inform search scheduling.
stance_target: Temporal patterns in lost subject activity can be modeled statistically to improve search timing
scope_conditions: ISRID data, wilderness SAR, human lost persons
quoted_support: Incident times follow a von Mises distribution centered near 5:30pm.

claim: Watershed boundaries provide a superior spatial prior to Euclidean distance rings for WiSAR probability maps because they reflect actual terrain barriers to travel, though the combined model outperforms either alone.
stance_target: Terrain-aware spatial models improve sighting-to-position inference over simple distance-ring models
quoted_support: Watershed boundaries follow high terrain and may better reflect actual barriers to travel. We also created a third model using the joint distribution using Euclidean and watershed features.
